coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode detection of ascorbic acid in orange juice by N/S doped carbon dots based on cellulose acetate
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wen
Li, Yan-feng
Lu, Qian
Qiao, Cong-de
Yang, Zhi-zhou
Zhang, Yun-qiang
Zhang, Xian
Zhao, Hui
coredata.dc:description: N/S-doped carbon dots/gold nanoparticles/Ag+ (N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+) as a dual-mode nanoprobe was constructed to detect ascorbic acid (AA). Compared with simple carbon dots, N/S-CDs exhibited superior fluorescence properties, which could play a more important role in building a sensitive homogenous FRET fluorescence detection platform. Moreover, AuNPs/Ag+ was reduced to core-shell Au@AgNPs by AA in an alkaline environment, which could induce the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and led to the change of UV absorption peak position from 520 nm to 426 nm with the color changing from pink to bright yellow. Due to the fluorescence “turn on-off” and LSPR, the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe is inspected for the sensitive dual-mode detection of AA. The linear detection ranges are from 0.5 to 100 μM for colorimetric mode and 0.5–90 μM for fluorescence method. Their LODs are 0.17 and 0.10 μM, respectively. Furthermore, this proposed nanoprobe showed high sensitivity and accuracy for AA detection in real orange juice, displaying great possibility in food testing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+
Ascorbic acid (AA)
Colorimetric
Fluorescence
Dual-mode nanoprobe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction dual-mode probe based on N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ for AA detection
The detection of AA in real samples
Construction of the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ dual-mode probe
Detection mechanism of the dual-mode sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shandong Province Technology oriented Small and Medium Enterprises Enhancement Project
Universities Twenty Foundational Items of Jinan City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Universities Twenty Foundational Items of Jinan City (Grant No. 2021GXRC097), Shandong Province Technology oriented Small and Medium Enterprises Enhancement Project (2023TSGC0575), and we are thanking for the help and cooperation from Xiang-hui Wu and Hong-xin Shang serving in Desheng Synthetic Materials Co. LTD. The Project Supported by the Talent research project of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant NO. 2023RCKY008) and Major innovation projects for integrating science, education & industry of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant NO. 2022JBZ01-07).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy image (TEM); (b) XRD spectrum; (c) FT-IR spectrum; (d) XPS total spectrum of N/S-CDs. Inset (a): The high-resolution TEM image of single N/S-CDs.

(a) Absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N/S-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N/S-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. The effects of pH value (c) and NaCl concentration (d) on the fluorescence of N/S-CDs.

TEM images of (a) AuNPs and (b) Au@AgNPs. The high-resolution TEM images of (c) N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ and (d) N/S-CDs/Au@AgNPs. (e) UV spectra and (f) fluorescence emission spectra of N/S-CDs in the presence or absence of AuNPs, Ag+ and AA.

(a) The UV spectra of colorimetric platform formed by adding different concentrations of AA (Inset: colors caused by different quantities of AA); (b) the absorbance fitting curve at 426 nm; (c) The fluorescence emission spectra of fluorescence platform formed by adding different quantities of AA (Inset: fluorescence caused by different quantities of AA under 365 nm UV light); (d) the fluorescence fitting curve at 443 nm.

The effect of different interfering substances on AA detection (a) in colorimetric nanoplatform and (b) in fluorescent nanoplatform. The recovery test results of AA (c) in colorimetric nanoplatform and (d) in fluorescent nanoplatform.
The preparation procedure and mechanism of N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ dual-mode sensor.
The comparison between the reported and our designed methods based on colorimetric and fluorometry detection for AA.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode detection of ascorbic acid in orange juice by N/S doped carbon dots based on cellulose acetate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N/S-doped carbon dots/gold nanoparticles/Ag+ (N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+) as a dual-mode nanoprobe was constructed to detect ascorbic acid (AA). Compared with simple carbon dots, N/S-CDs exhibited superior fluorescence properties, which could play a more important role in building a sensitive homogenous FRET fluorescence detection platform. Moreover, AuNPs/Ag+ was reduced to core-shell Au@AgNPs by AA in an alkaline environment, which could induce the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and led to the change of UV absorption peak position from 520 nm to 426 nm with the color changing from pink to bright yellow. Due to the fluorescence “turn on-off” and LSPR, the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe is inspected for the sensitive dual-mode detection of AA. The linear detection ranges are from 0.5 to 100 μM for colorimetric mode and 0.5–90 μM for fluorescence method. Their LODs are 0.17 and 0.10 μM, respectively. Furthermore, this proposed nanoprobe showed high sensitivity and accuracy for AA detection in real orange juice, displaying great possibility in food testing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA) is a vital substance to maintain the physiological function of the human body. However, due to the lack of key enzymes, human subjects are unable to produce AA and can only obtain it from fresh fruits and vegetables [1]. Insufficient or superfluous intake of AA results in adverse reactions such as anemia and decreased resistance, and may be life-threatening in severe cases [2]. To ensure the appropriate amount intake of AA every day, detecting the content of AA in food is necessary. Therefore, it is crucial to construct simple and accurate probes for AA.

The conventional detection methods for AA, such as electrochemical method [3], titration [4], high performance liquid chromatography [5], are complicated and time-consuming. However, colorimetry is intuitive and simple, and can be used for the on-site detection by color change [6–9]. The fluorescence detection is rapid, highly sensitive and selective, and has been broadly applied [10–12]. Recently, dual-mode probes have attracted widespread attention in the field of analytical detection because of enhancing the reliability the results through mutual verification of two signals, while saving time, labor, and reagents and showing greater flexibility [13,14]. The colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode probe, which combines the dual advantages of colorimetry and fluorescence, is the most widely used [15–17].

The current probes in the dual-mode detection have some disadvantages, for example, the higher limits of detection [18], additional chromogenic substrates [16], the PH instability of the probe, no laddered color change [19], the low quantum yields, dangerous and harmful chemical reagents as the raw materials [20], and so on. It is essential to prepare probes with excellent performance. Carbon dots (CDs) display low cost, flexibility of surface modification, high dispersion in water, good light stability, high sensitivity, and excellent biocompatibility, making them widely used in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based “turn on-off” fluorescence sensing systems [21–23]. Cellulose is a cheap, environmentally friendly, renewable and easily chemically modified biomass material [24], and cellulose-based CDs have excellent optical properties and extended π-conjugated structure [25,26]. Therefore, the rational design of functional cellulose-based CDs with more stable and efficient photoluminescence properties, making them suitable for AA dual-modal detection, remains a crucial point.

In this paper, N/S-doped cellulose-based CDs (N/S-CDs) were prepared, and the synthetic details were discussed in order to get better fluorescence properties of N/S-CDs. Moreover, considering the reducibility of AA [27,28], colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode probes for AA detection were constructed based on the localized surface plasmon resonance of absorption peak (Scheme 1 -Ⅰ) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Scheme 1-Ⅱ). In addition, the sensitivity and specificity of N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ for AA were explored. Lastly, the practicability of the detection platform in real samples was investigated. It is worth noting that the designed detection platform base on N/S-CDs is made of environmentally friendly and low toxic raw materials, do not require additional chromogenic substrates to produce laddered color changes and has been successfully applied to the detection of AA in orange juice.

Cellulose acetate (Acetyl 39.8 wt%, Hydroxyl 3.5 wt%), melamine, silver nitrate (AgNO3) and chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7), sodium thiosulfate and glycine (Gly) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). The other chemicals were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China) without any further purification before use. All solutions used in the experiments were prepared with ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) obtained through the STU4100 purification system (Ulupure, China).

UV spectra were obtained by a UV-3600 UV spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured by an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The chemical composition of N/S-CDs was performed by a Nicolet 10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). XPS spectra were recorded by ESCALABXi + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The particle size of N/S-CDs was carried out on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern, UK). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were observed through a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL Ltd, Musashino, Japan). The high-resolution TEM images were observed through a JEM-F200 electron microscope (Ultim Max 80, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) images were acquired by a D8-ADVANCE diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany).

N/S-CDs were synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method. The preparation process was optimized by investigating the ratio of melamine to sodium thiosulfate, as well as the reaction time and temperature.

First step: Cellulose acetate, sodium thiosulfate and different masses of melamine were mixed in 35 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonically dispersed for 40 min. Then the mixture was transferred into a 50 mL of poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave, heated to 180 °C and kept for 21 h. The fluorescence intensities of different samples were measured at 443 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Second step: The mass of melamine was fixed through the above experiment. Different masses of sodium thiosulfate were dispersed into 35 ml of ultrapure water along with cellulose acetate and sodium thiosulfate. All other conditions were the same as above.
Third step: The mass of sodium thiosulfate was fixed through the second experiment. The reaction was performed at different temperatures under keeping all other conditions the same.
Fourth step: The reaction temperature was fixed through the above experiment. The reaction time was varied under keeping all other conditions constant.

The N/S-CDs obtained under the above optimized conditions were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min, and then dialyzed (1000 Da retained molecular weight) for 48 h. Finally, the N/S-CDs solution was freeze-dried, and the resultant brown powder was kept at 4 °C for the next experiments.

Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M of dilute sulfuric acid (QY = 0.56, η = 1.33) as the standard substance to calculate QY following the blow formula (1) [29]:
(1)
QY x
=
QY st
(
η x
η st
)
2
(
A st
A x
)
(
F x
F st
)
Where QYx and QYst represent QY of N/S-CDs and quinine sulfate. ηx and ηst indicate the solvent refractive index of N/S-CDs and quinine sulfate. Ax and Ast are the absorbance of N/S-CDs and quinine sulfate. Fx and Fst denote the integral areas of the fluorescence spectra of N/S-CDs and quinine sulfate.

AuNPs were prepared by the citric acid reduction method using HAuCl4·4H2O and sodium citrate as raw materials. HAuCl4·4H2O solution (0.5 mL, 50 mM) was mixed with ultrapure water (100 mL), stirred and heated. After severe boiling, sodium citrate aqueous solution (5 mL, 34 mM) was quickly added into the mixture. The obtained solution was continuously heated until the color turned wine red, indicating the formation of AuNPs. After heating for another 10 min, the AuNPs with the appropriate particle size were naturally cooled to room temperature, and then centrifuged to remove the supernatant. The prepared nanoparticles were redispersed in ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction dual-mode probe based on N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ for AA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The influencing factors of dual-mode probes especially fluorescent mode probes, including the pH and concentration of Gly-NaOH buffer, the ratio of AuNPs and AgNO3, incubation time and temperature were studied.
The pH and concentration of Gly-NaOH buffer:

AuNPs (200 μL, 50 μg/mL), AgNO3 (50 μL, 10 mM) and AA (50 μL, 2 mM) were adjusted to 1.9 mL by Gly-NaOH buffer (1.6 mL, 19 mM) with different pH (8.0, 8.5, 9.0, 9.5, 10.0, 10.5). After incubation for 10 min at 25 °C, N/S-CDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) were added and the fluorescence was recorded.
The concentration of Gly-NaOH buffer (6, 13, 19, 25, 31, 38 mM) was changed under pH = 9.0, and all other conditions were the same as above.
The ratio of AuNPs and AgNO3:

AuNPs (200 μL, 50 μg/mL), AA (50 μL, 2 mM) and different concentrations of AgNO3 (50 μL, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 mM) were adjusted to 1.9 mL by Gly-NaOH buffer (19 mM, pH = 9.0). After incubation for 10 min at 25 °C, N/S-CDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) were added and the fluorescence was recorded.
AgNO3 was determined to be 10 mM by the above experiment. The concentrations of AuNPs (13, 25, 38, 50, 63, 75 μg/mL) were further changed, and the same process was finished as the above.
Incubation time and temperature:

AuNPs (200 μL, 50 μg/mL), AgNO3 (50 μL, 10 mM) and AA (50 μL, 2 mM) were adjusted to 1.9 mL by Gly-NaOH buffer (19 mM, pH = 9.0). After incubating different times (6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 min) at 25 °C, N/S-CDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) were added and the fluorescence was recorded.

The incubation time was determined as 10 min by the above experiment. The different incubation temperature (20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 °C) was changed, and then the optimized temperature was obtained by the fluorescence change.

The mechanism of N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ dual-mode probe was investigated. For the colorimetric platform, the samples with different concentrations of AA (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200 μM) were separately mixed with AuNPs (200 μL, 50 μg/mL), AgNO3 (50 μL, 10 mM) and Gly-NaOH buffer (19 mM, pH 9.0). The mixed solution was incubated for 10 min at 25 °C. The color change and UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures were collected, respectively. In addition, to further improve the detection sensitivity, N/S-CDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) were added into the above system to build the fluorescence detection platform. And the fluorescent signal changes were collected (excitation 360 nm). The selectivity of the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe to AA was evaluated with other possible interferences, including 500 μmol/L of sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2·4H2O), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) and 150 μmol/L of l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-alanine (L-Ala), l-lysine (L-Lys), leucine (Leu), citric acid, l-tryptophan (L-Try), dl-serine methyl ester hydrochloride (DL-Ser), l-histidine (L-His), respectively. Then the UV–vis absorption and fluorescent signal changes were recorded, respectively. All colorimetric and fluorescent experiments were conducted under the same conditions as the AA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of AA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh oranges were bought in the supermarket (Jinan, China), then peeled and juiced. The juice was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm water-based membrane. Following the previous procedure, the AA content in orange juice was tested. Furthermore, different concentrations of AA (20, 50, 70 μM) were added and fully mixed with orange juice diluted 50 times and 25 times. The mixed samples were detected by the dual-mode platform to record their UV absorption and fluorescence. Lastly, the spike recovery rates of AA were calculated.

N/S-CDs were synthesized by a regular one-step hydrothermal method [30]. To obtain the N/S-CDs with better fluorescence properties, some influencing factors including the ratio of melamine and sodium thiosulfate, reaction time and temperature were studied. Firstly, the amount of sodium thiosulfate was fixed to adjust the amount of melamine (0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9 g), it was found that the obtained N/S-CDs exhibited the strongest fluorescence at the melamine of 0.7 g (Fig. S1a). This data showed that the fluorescence of CDs was enhanced because N was imported by melamine. However, too much N will produce defects and reduce the fluorescence [31]. Similarly, if fixing the amount of melamine, the optimal amount of sodium thiosulfate can be obtained as 0.4 g (Fig. S1b). Moreover, the different reaction times (15, 18, 21, 24, 27 h) were studied (Fig. S1c), it was found that the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs firstly raised and then reduced, reaching the maximum at the reaction time of 21 h. This may be due to too short or too long preparation time to easily form uniform particles [29]. When the other factors were constant, the fluorescence intensity increased with the rise of temperature and became steady after 180 °C (Fig. S1d), which may be due to the incomplete structure at lower temperature [32]. Under the above optimized conditions (cellulose acetate 1 g, melamine 0.7 g, sodium thiosulfate 0.4 g, 21 h, 180 °C), the obtained N/S-CDs showed the optimum fluorescence properties.

In Fig. 1 a, TEM images of the optimized N/S-CDs indicate that N/S-CDs are spherical and well dispersed in water. The particle size distribution is from 3.5 to 6.5 nm with an average diameter of 5.6 nm in Fig. S2. A lattice space of 0.31 nm corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite crystal structure, which is similar to the previous report [33]. Furthermore, the XRD spectrum of N/S-CDs shows a wide diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25o (Fig. 1b), and its interplanar spacing (0.356 nm) is close to the value of the (002) crystal plane (0.32 nm) of the graphite crystal structure. There is no obvious sharp diffraction peak in the XRD diffraction pattern, which demonstrates that the N/S-CDs are mainly amorphous carbon structures without complete and continuous crystal structure [23].

For FT-IR spectrum of N/S-CDs in Fig. 1c, one can see that the absorption peaks at ∼3400 cm−1, ∼3225 cm−1, ∼1530 cm−1 and ∼1440 cm−1 ascribe O–H, N–H, CN and C–N stretching vibrations [34], respectively. The peaks at ∼1733 cm−1 and ∼1658 cm−1 are assigned to CO stretching vibrations. In the 1028∼1202 cm−1 region, the absorption bands belong to C–O, SO and S–O stretching vibrations. Under the 1000 cm−1 region, the band at ∼770 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption of the bending vibration of triazine ring. And the peak at ∼525 cm−1 belongs to S–S stretching vibrations.

The elementary composition, content and surface groups of N/S-CDs are also confirmed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum, and Fig. 1d shows the existence of elements C, O, N and S with atom ratios of 63.07 %, 13.68 %, 17.15 % and 6.11 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C1s in Fig. S3a display three different types of surface components with C–C/CC (284.4 eV), C–N/C–O (285.1 eV) and CO/CN (288.6 eV). The binding energy at 401.5 eV, 400.63 eV and 399.35 eV (Fig. S3b) correspond to N–H (amino groups), pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively. And the S3p spectra (Fig. S3c) present the three peaks relating to SO (161.5 eV), C–S3p3/2 (163.7 eV) and C–S3p1/2 (165.26 eV), which indicates that S is doped into N/S-CDs by sulfonate state. The peaks at 531.2 eV, 532.0 eV and 536.0 eV in the O1s spectra (Fig. S3d) belong to CO, C–OH/C–O–C and SO [29,35], respectively. All FT-IR and XPS data demonstrate the successful preparation of N/S-CDs with some hydrophilic groups, including –OH, –COOH and –NH2, which could improve the dispersion of N/S-CDs in aqueous solution, and the feasibility for the AA detection.

In addition, the N/S-CDs emit brilliant blue-green fluorescence in the inset of Fig. 2 a under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp with a fluorescence quantum yield of 21.8 %. The UV–vis spectrum of the N/S-CDs in Fig. 2a indicates a characteristic π–π* stacking transition of aromatic sp2 domains (UV ∼260 nm). The fluorescence excitation peak at ∼360 nm belongs to the n-π* transition of CO/C–NH2 on the surface of N/S-CDs [36]. The fluorescence emission spectrum exhibits that N/S-CDs (ex: 360 nm, em: 443 nm, I: 2590 cd) present a significantly strengthened fluorescence and a red-shift emission wavelength in comparison to the simple CDs (ex: 350 nm, em: 433 nm, I: 715 cd) (Fig. S4). The excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristic was shown in Fig. 2b, consistent well with the results reported previously [30].

Meanwhile, the effects of pH and salt environment on fluorescence of N/S-CDs were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs weakly hinged on the pH value of surrounding environments. Within the range of 2∼12, the fluorescence intensity remains relatively stable, while it weakens under extreme alkaline or acidic conditions (Fig. 2c). With the rise of NaCl concentration (0∼1.0 mM), the fluorescent intensity of N/S-CDs is almost unchanged, indicating that the N/S-CDs present high salt tolerance (Fig. 2d). In general, the obtained N/S-CDs have unexceptionable fluorescence stability, acid and alkali resistance and salt resistance, giving them great potential in AA detection and biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ dual-mode probe
ce:section.ce:para: The construction of the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ dual-mode probe involved several factors, including the concentration and pH of Gly-NaOH buffer, the ratio of AuNPs to AgNO3, incubation time, and temperature. The fluorescent quenching efficiency (ɳ=(F0–F)/F0, where F0 and F represent the fluorescent intensity of the system without AA and with AA) was used as a criterion for the evaluation of each factor.

Firstly, the pH and concentration of Gly-NaOH buffer were explored (Fig. S5a). At pH = 9.0, the quenching efficiency reaches the largest (55 %). It is due to the fact that the reducing ability of AA increases with the rise of pH, but when the pH is so high, the stability of AgNO3 decreases, which is not conducive to the formation of Au@AgNPs [37]. As the concentration of Gly in Gly-NaOH buffers increases, the quenching efficiency increases (Fig. S5b). When Gly reaches 19 mM, the quenching efficiency attains the maximum value of 53.3 % and then begins to decrease. Because an appropriate concentration of Gly makes the pH environment of the buffer more stable, but a high concentration of Gly can cause the aggregation of AuNPs [38], affecting the quenching efficiency.

The concentration of AgNO3 may affect the particle size and morphology of AgNPs, and thus lead to changes in the LSPR peak of Au@AgNPs. Fig. S5c shows that the maximum quenching efficiency is 52.6 % when the Ag+ concentration is 10 mM. The quenching efficiency rises with the increase of AuNPs concentration and tends to be stable when the concentration is 50 μg/mL (Fig. S5d). Moreover, the investigation of incubation time (Fig. S5e) and temperature (Fig. S5f) found that 10 min and 25 °C can achieve the maximum quenching efficiency, respectively.

To sum up the above, the sensitive dual-mode probe for the detection of AA is constructed. The preferred conditions are as follows: pH = 9, 19 mM of Gly in buffer, the concentration of 50 μg/mL of AuNPs, 10 mM of AgNO3, N/S-CDs (100 μL, 0.3 mg/mL) incubating at 25 °C for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of the dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs (200 μL, 50 μg/mL), AgNO3 (50 μL, 10 mM) and 1.6 mL of Gly-NaOH buffer (19 mM, PH 9.0) were mixed together. The TEM image of pure AuNPs in Fig. 3 a indicates that they are stable dispersed nanoparticles. Fig. 3b is the TEM image of Au@AgNPs obtained by adding AA (50 μL, 2 mM) to AuNPs/Ag+ and blending at 25 °C for 10 min. The nanoparticles are obvious with a core-shell structure, which is due to that Ag+ can be reduced to Ag by AA under alkaline conditions [39]. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM images of N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ in the presence and absence of AA are showed in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. We can see that AuNPs can uniformly coexist with N/S-CDs and Ag+, and the particle size of the system is about three times that of N/S-CDs. After AA (50 μL, 2 mM) was added to the system of N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ and blended at 25 °C for 10 min, and the TEM image in Fig. 3d more clearly displays that Ag+ is reduced to Ag by AA and further attach to the surface of AuNPs to obtain core-shell nanoparticles of N/S-CDs/Au@AgNPs.

As a result, the LSPR of Au@AgNPs changes, ultimately causing the color of the system to transform from pink to bright yellow (Fig. 4 a-inset). Correspondingly, Fig. 4a showed that the UV–vis absorption peak of the AuNPs at 520 nm gradually superseded to the peak of Au@AgNPs at 426 nm with the increased AA concentrations due to the reduction of the Ag+. The redox process can be simply illustrated by the following reaction equation (2), and the similar descriptions were reported in the other literatures [40–42]:
(2)
2 Ag
+
+
C 6
H 8
O 6
→
2 Ag
0
+
C 6
H 6
O 6
+
2 H
+

Meanwhile, the AuNPs can be turned into Au@AgNPs nanoparticles in the presence of Ag+ and AA (Fig. 3e). Due to the high overlay between the emission band of N/S-CDs and the UV spectrum of Au@AgNPs (Fig. S6), the emission fluorescence of N/S-CDs can be quenched by Au@AgNPs through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. 3f). Hence, the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe can be devoted to detect AA with colorimetric and fluorescence dual-modes.

The detection of AA using N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ fluorescence nanoprobe was studied. The AuNPs (50 μg/mL) and AgNO3 (10 mM) were incubated with AA aqueous solutions with various concentrations for 10 min and the N/S-CDs were added. Then fluorescent spectra of the probes incubated with AA solutions are displayed in Fig. 4c. The results prove that the fluorescent intensity decreases as the concentration of AA enlargement, which is contributed to the amount increase of the Au@AgNPs nanoparticles. Correspondingly, the inset of Fig. 4c showed that the fluorescence of N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ was gradually quenched with the addition of AA under 365 nm UV light. The calibration plots of the detection (Fig. 4d) present that the fluorescent intensity increases linearly with the rise of AA concentrations in the range of 0.5–90 μM. The detection limit (LOD) is 0.10 μM, which is calculated as 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio. We also detected AA at different concentrations using UV spectrophotometry based on the AuNPs/Ag+ system. The results exhibited that in the concentration range of 0.5–100 μM, the UV absorbance increases linearly with the rise of AA concentration and LOD is 0.17 μM (Fig. 4b). These data further suggest that the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe can supply highly sensitive detection for AA. Compared with previously reported UV spectrophotometry and fluorescence detection systems (Table 1 ), the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe has also revealed high detection performance for AA.

To estimate the selectivity of the earthly dual-mode sensor toward AA, the nanoprobe towards other possible interferences was examined, including various metal ions (Na+, Cl+, Mg+, Ca+, Fe+) with a concentration of 500 μM and weak reducing bioagents (L-Cys, L-Ala, L-Lys, Leu, citric acid, L-Try, DL-Ser, L-His) with a concentration of 150 μM. All experiments were executed under the same conditions of AA detection. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, when the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe was incubated with AA at the concentrations of 50 μM, a major increase in the UV absorbance and fluorescence intensity was observed. However, no significant changes were found with the interferences, even including the citric acid and L-Cys. The data confirms that the N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe has outstanding specificity toward AA and can be used in the exact detection of AA in the existence of other interference molecules.

As nanoprobes are of practical significance, actual sample detectability evaluation is necessary. The N/S-CDs/AuNPs/Ag+ nanoprobe was used to detect AA with orange juice. After being diluted 50 and 25 times, the concentration of AA was measured as 5 μM and 10 μM. Then, the standard addition experiments were implemented. The different concentrations of AA (20 μM, 50 μM and 70 μM) were added to the above diluted samples, respectively. The recovery rates of the colorimetric platform ranged from 98.25 % to 102.57 % (Fig. 5c), and that of the fluorescence platform ranged from 98.02 % to 101.48 % (Fig. 5d). It verifies that the dual-mode platform is good accuracy and can be applied accurately in actual samples.

In this system, using sodium thiosulfate, melamine and cellulose acetate as sulfur sources, nitrogen sources, and carbon sources respectively, blue-green fluorescent N/S-CDs were produced by the one-step hydrothermal method under optimal conditions. N/S-CDs exhibited good dispersion in water and its fluorescence intensity was 3.6 times that of simple CDs, which was beneficial to improve the sensitivity of the detection platform. In alkaline environments, Ag+ can be reduced to Ag by AA and the Ag can affix to the skin of AuNPs to become core-shell Au@AgNPs, which would result in a change of LSPR and manifest as changes in color and UV absorbance. Meanwhile, because of the FRET between Au@AgNPs and N/S-CDs, the fluorescence detection platform for AA was constructed. For this dual-modal detection platform, the linear range in UV mode was 0.5–100 μM and the LOD was 0.17 μM. The linear range in fluorescence mode was 0.5–90 μM and the LOD was 0.10 μM. The dual-modal detection platform also presented excellent selectivity toward AA, and the standard recovery experiment for orange juice showed the high accuracy of the platform in real samples. A suitable method is proposed that can serve as a convenient platform for ascorbic acid detection in food testing applications.

Wen Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Yan-feng Li: Data curation. Qian Lu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Cong-de Qiao: Resources, Funding acquisition. Zhi-zhou Yang: Data curation. Yun-qiang Zhang: Validation. Xian Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Hui Zhao: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Universities Twenty Foundational Items of Jinan City (Grant No. 2021GXRC097), Shandong Province Technology oriented Small and Medium Enterprises Enhancement Project (2023TSGC0575), and we are thanking for the help and cooperation from Xiang-hui Wu and Hong-xin Shang serving in Desheng Synthetic Materials Co. LTD. The Project Supported by the Talent research project of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant NO. 2023RCKY008) and Major innovation projects for integrating science, education & industry of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant NO. 2022JBZ01-07).
